title: Why stretching can feel threatening to a dysregulated nervous system
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
You tried to stretch and your body said no

I remember lying on a yoga mat once, early in my recovery, trying to do a simple hamstring stretch. My instructor was saying something about breathing into the discomfort. I was trying. I really was. But my hamstrings weren't having it. They felt like steel cables. Every time I leaned forward, my whole body locked up tighter. I left feeling like I had failed at something so basic. Like I couldn't even stretch right.

Here's the thing. I wasn't failing. My nervous system was doing exactly what it was designed to do. It was protecting me. And it was telling me something important that I wasn't ready to hear yet.

If you've ever felt threatened by stretching, or if your body clenches up when you try to lengthen a muscle, you're not broken. You're not doing it wrong. You're not weak. Your nervous system is simply reading the stretch as a signal of danger. And it's responding the only way it knows how. By pulling you back into safety.

Stay with me here. This gets interesting.

What stretching actually asks of your nervous system

When you stretch a muscle, you're asking your body to do something counterintuitive from a survival standpoint. You're asking it to lengthen. To open. To release tension that may have been holding you together for years. Think about it. From your nervous system's perspective, tension isn't just a physical sensation. It's a strategy. A protection mechanism. A way of keeping you safe from something it perceives as threatening.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist and researcher behind The Body Keeps the Score, spent decades showing us that trauma lives in the body. It's not just a story in your head. It's a physical imprint. Your muscles, your fascia, your connective tissue all hold patterns of protection that developed in response to overwhelming experiences. When you try to stretch those patterns, you're asking your body to let go of a survival strategy it has been relying on.

And your body doesn't know that you're just trying to touch your toes. It thinks you're trying to dismantle its defenses.

So it fights back. It tightens. It pulls away. It sends signals of pain or discomfort that feel disproportionate to what you're actually doing. This isn't weakness. This is wisdom. Your body is saying, "I don't feel safe enough to let go yet."

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine. He explains how animals in the wild instinctively shake off trauma, while humans often freeze and hold tension instead. Understanding that mechanism changes everything about how you approach your body.

The polyvagal lens on physical tension

Stephen Porges developed polyvagal theory, and it's one of the most useful frameworks I've ever encountered for understanding why stretching can feel threatening. Porges showed us that your nervous system has three main states. Ventral vagal, which is your social engagement and safety state. Sympathetic, which is your fight or flight state. And dorsal vagal, which is your freeze or collapse state.

When you're in a sympathetic or dorsal state, your muscles are already primed for action or shutdown. They're holding tension as a preparation for survival. Stretching, in that context, feels like an attack on that preparation. Your nervous system reads the lengthening as a vulnerability. As an opening that could be exploited by a predator. So it resists.

This is why forcing a stretch never works long term. You might get temporary flexibility gains, but your nervous system will just tighten back up as soon as you stop. Because the underlying threat perception hasn't changed. You haven't convinced your body that it's safe to let go.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana. She offers concrete ways to work with your nervous system states instead of against them. It's a game changer for anyone who has ever felt like their body is betraying them.

I want you to notice something. When you try to stretch and your body resists, where do you feel it? Is it a sharp pain? A dull ache? A sense of panic? A desire to stop immediately? That information is valuable. Your body is telling you exactly where its protective patterns live. And that's the first step in working with them instead of against them.

How to approach stretching when you're dysregulated

So what do you do when stretching feels threatening? The answer isn't to push harder. It's not to breathe through the pain. It's not to tell yourself that you're being dramatic. The wellness industry has done a lot of damage by promoting this idea that discomfort equals growth. Sometimes discomfort equals danger. And your body knows the difference, even if your mind doesn't.

Here's what actually works. You start smaller. Much smaller. Instead of trying to stretch a muscle to its full length, you find the very beginning of the sensation. The place where you first notice that something is happening. And you stop there. You don't go further. You just hang out at that edge and let your nervous system get used to the idea that nothing bad is happening.

You can even move away from the stretch slightly. Then come back. Pendulation, Peter Levine calls it. Moving between contraction and expansion, between tension and release, in tiny increments that your nervous system can tolerate. This builds safety gradually. It teaches your body that lengthening doesn't have to mean danger.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate. Magnesium plays a role in muscle relaxation and nervous system regulation. It's not a cure, but it can help create a baseline of calm that makes stretching feel less threatening.

Another approach is to change your relationship with the stretch entirely. Instead of thinking of it as something you do to your body, think of it as something you ask your body if it's willing to do. You become a curious observer rather than a demanding instructor. You ask, "Is it okay if we explore this area together?" And you respect the answer, even if the answer is no.

I've found that lying on the floor and doing very gentle, supported stretches is much less threatening than standing or sitting upright. When your body feels supported by the ground, your nervous system can shift toward safety more easily. A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored by Deb Dana. It offers exercises that help you find that sense of safety in your body before you even attempt stretching.

Why your history matters here

Your nervous system doesn't exist in a vacuum. It carries the weight of everything that has happened to you. If you have a history of physical trauma, of being held down, of being restrained, of being hurt in your body, then stretching can trigger those memories. Not consciously, necessarily. But your body remembers. And it responds accordingly.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who integrates structural dissociation theory with somatic approaches, talks about how different parts of us can have different relationships with the body. One part might want to stretch and feel flexible. Another part might be terrified of any movement that feels like surrender. Both parts are valid. Both parts need to be heard.

This is why stretching can feel like an internal war. You have one part of you that wants to heal and another part that is trying to keep you safe. And both are fighting for control of the same body. The resolution isn't to defeat the protective part. It's to listen to it. To thank it for keeping you alive. And to slowly, patiently, show it that the danger has passed.

Wild, right? That your hamstring tightness might actually be a conversation between different parts of your psyche. But it is. And once you start seeing it that way, everything changes.

The slow path to safety

I'm not going to tell you that if you just do these things, stretching will become easy. It might not. For some of us, the nervous system's protective patterns are deeply entrenched. They took years to develop. They won't dissolve overnight. And that's okay. There's no deadline for healing.

What I can tell you is that every time you honor your body's resistance instead of fighting it, you build a little more trust. Every time you stop at the edge of discomfort instead of pushing through, your nervous system learns that you are a safe companion. That you won't force it into vulnerability before it's ready.

And that trust is the foundation for everything else. Not just stretching. But for feeling at home in your own body. For being able to rest. For being able to connect with others. For being able to live without constant vigilance.

So if stretching feels threatening right now, don't stretch. Or stretch in ways that feel safe. Or just lie on the floor and breathe. Or do nothing at all. Your body knows what it needs. And it will let you know when it's ready to open.

Sit with that.
